headline: LSU's ground game overpowers Miami despite Mertz's aerial clinic
body: Garrett Nussmeier didn't need to be perfect to beat Miami on Saturday. The LSU quarterback managed his game with surgical efficiency, completing 15 of 26 passes for 204 yards and two touchdowns while the Tigers leaned hard on their rushing attack. Ferguson and company accumulated 181 yards on the ground, establishing a physical identity that Miami's defense simply couldn't match. Nussmeier's poise in the pocket—particularly his ball security with only one turnover against Miami's two fumbles—proved the difference in a game where field position and execution mattered more than statistical volume.

Miami's Cam Mertz put on an impressive individual performance, torching LSU for 322 passing yards and two scores on 20 completions. His receivers, led by the dynamic Dike with 103 yards, created explosive plays downfield. Yet those big plays came too often on third and long, a symptom of Miami's inability to sustain drives on the ground. Lilly rushed for 79 yards but never found a rhythm, and the Hurricanes' defense couldn't generate enough pressure up front. Dowell's sack and five tackles were scrappy, but it wasn't enough to disrupt the Tigers' methodical approach.

The momentum tilt came late in the third quarter when LSU's defense forced Miami into back-to-back three-and-outs after the Hurricanes had closed the gap to a one-possession game. Nussmeier then led a controlled scoring drive that put the game out of reach. Miami's turnovers—two fumbles lost versus LSU's zero—told the story of who was more poised under pressure. For all of Mertz's arm talent, he couldn't overcome the foundational issues in Miami's run game and ball security that plagued the Hurricanes from start to finish.
turningPoint: Late third quarter when LSU forced consecutive Miami three-and-outs and answered with a touchdown drive that extended the lead from three to eleven points, effectively ending Miami's comeback threat.

unsungHero.name: Javon Ferguson
unsungHero.why: The LSU running back's 13 carries and one touchdown anchored the Tigers' ground-oriented game plan, controlling tempo and field position in ways that never show up as highlight plays but were absolutely critical to wearing down Miami's defense.
